title: Meet Claude
promise: Study it, prove it, keep it. The whole first module — what Claude is, how to talk to it, and which mode fits the job — with a question before every answer and a quiz that remembers what you missed.

loop.blurb: Read each idea after you've tried to predict it.
Say it back in your own words before moving on.
Take the quiz — recognition, with hints on.
Come back; the misses resurface first.

map.blurb: The principles, generate-not-retrieve, the context window, and the many doors in.
The Stage / Task / Rules prompt, uploads as context, and iterating instead of restarting.
Chat, Cowork, Code — what each is for and how to pick by the size of the job.

map.stop: The moves that make it useful
map.blurb: Troubleshooting, the 4 D's of AI Fluency, and evals you can run yourself.

intro: You've used a chatbot. You typed a question, got a wall of text back, and half of it missed the point. So the question isn't "what can Claude do" — it's "why does mine give me junk while someone else gets exactly what they wanted." The gap is almost never the tool. It's a handful of things about how Claude actually works that nobody told you up front.

This guide walks the 13 objectives in Module 1, lesson by lesson, plus the one idea from the AI Fluency video that's worth more than the rest combined. Each section names the thing, shows you one concrete example, and tells you the exact sentence you should be able to say back to prove you've got it. Read it once tonight and you'll stop fighting the tool and start steering it.

sections.heading: Three letters run the whole thing
sections.challenge: What three words govern how Claude behaves — and what's the method that trains them in?
body: Claude is an AI assistant built to be a thinking partner, and three words govern how it behaves: helpful, harmless, honest. Helpful means it tries to do what you actually asked. Harmless means it declines to help with things that hurt people. Honest means it won't dress up a guess as a fact.

Those three aren't bolted on after training. They come from Constitutional AI — Claude is trained against a written set of values, a kind of rulebook, instead of being shaped only by humans rating answers one at a time. That's why its behavior is fairly steerable and predictable. You're talking to something built to a stated standard, not a black box that drifts.
sections.plain: Most models are trained on a million thumbs-up/thumbs-down clicks; Constitutional AI gives Claude a written rulebook to check itself against.
sections.lockIt: Say the three H's — helpful, harmless, honest — and define Constitutional AI in one sentence: training Claude to align with a written set of human values rather than raw human feedback alone.

sections.heading: It generates, it doesn't look up
sections.challenge: Does Claude look up your answer or compose it? And what is the 'context window'?
body: A simple chatbot retrieves. You ask, it finds the closest canned answer in a list, it hands that back. Claude doesn't work that way. It generates a fresh response every time, word by word, built for your exact request. Ask the same thing twice and you'll get two different phrasings — because nothing was pulled from a drawer.

The other half of what makes it more than a chatbot is the context window: everything Claude can see at once. Roughly 200,000 tokens — call it 500 pages — and up to a million on the higher plans. That's the working memory it reasons over: your instructions, the chat so far, any files you pasted. Big window means you can hand it a whole report and ask questions across all of it. On top of that sit the real capabilities — writing, research, coding, step-by-step reasoning, and a learning mode that walks you through your own thinking instead of just handing you the answer.
sections.plain: Claude is a writer composing a new answer, not a librarian fetching a pre-written one — and the context window is how big a desk it can spread your materials across.
sections.lockIt: Explain why "Claude looks up answers from a database" is false (it generates, doesn't retrieve), and say what the context window is: everything Claude can hold in view at once, ~200K tokens / ~500 pages, up to 1M on Pro and above.

sections.heading: One Claude, many doors
sections.challenge: Name three different places you could reach Claude from.
body: There aren't several Claudes. There's one, reachable through different surfaces. The web app at claude.ai is the main door and the focus of this course. There's a desktop app and a mobile app. There's Claude Code, the agentic tool that edits files and runs commands on your machine. And there's the API for building Claude into your own software. Two more live where work already happens: Claude in Slack and Claude for Excel.

When you're signed in, your conversations, projects, memory, and preferences sync across devices. Start a chat on your phone, finish it on your laptop. The surface changes; the assistant and its context don't.
sections.plain: Think of it like one email account you can open from a browser, a phone app, or a desktop client — same inbox, different windows onto it.
sections.lockIt: Name three surfaces you can reach Claude through — for example web, desktop, mobile (Claude Code and the API also count).

sections.heading: A new chat wipes the desk clean
sections.challenge: Start a new chat — what carries over, and what doesn't?
body: The interface is mostly the sidebar. New chats up top, your old ones below so you can jump back in. Projects group related conversations and give them persistent context and custom instructions. Artifacts are the panel where a draft, a tool, or a small app shows up as something you can edit and share.

The one thing worth burning into memory: a new chat is a fresh context window. Claude carries nothing over from the last conversation except what Memory has saved. So when a chat gets cluttered or goes off the rails, starting fresh isn't losing progress — it's clearing the desk so the next answer isn't buried under old noise.
sections.plain: A new chat is a blank whiteboard, not the same board erased — last conversation's scribbles aren't faintly still there.
sections.lockIt: Start a new conversation from the sidebar and state what resets when you do: the context window — Claude sees none of the previous chat's contents (only saved Memory persists).

sections.heading: Stage, task, rules
sections.challenge: Name the three parts every strong prompt should have.
body: The single biggest lever on output quality is how you frame the ask, and there's a three-part shape for it. Set the stage: your role, your objective, the context Claude should know. Define the task: the one action you want — write, analyze, build. Specify the rules: tone, length, format, examples of what good looks like.

Watch it work on a real prompt. "I'm the marketing lead at an indie streaming startup preparing a Series A pitch deck" — that's the stage. "Research the independent film streaming market and identify key trends, competitor positioning, and growth opportunities" — that's the task. "Use current web research with citations, structure it as a professional report up to 5 pages with an executive summary, market analysis, competitive landscape, and growth opportunities" — those are the rules. Talk to Claude like a sharp coworker: natural, specific, no robot-speak required.
sections.plain: Stage tells Claude who's asking and why, task tells it what to do, rules tell it what good looks like — drop any one and it starts guessing.
sections.lockIt: Name the three parts of a strong prompt — setting the stage, defining the task, specifying the rules — and label them in a prompt you write yourself.

sections.heading: Uploading is a context shortcut
sections.challenge: Why hand Claude a file instead of describing it?
body: Instead of describing a document in three paragraphs, attach it. Claude reads PDF, DOCX, CSV, TXT, and images like PNG and JPEG. It parses text and the visuals — charts, graphs, screenshots — inside them. The file shows up as an attachment, Claude takes it in, then you prompt about it.

This is the fastest way to give Claude what it needs to be accurate instead of generic. Summarize a contract, find the trend in a spreadsheet, explain code, spot the bug in a screenshot. You're not making Claude work harder; you're handing it the context it would otherwise have to guess at.
sections.plain: Pasting the actual file beats describing it — same reason you'd show a contractor the room instead of telling him its dimensions over the phone.
sections.lockIt: Say what uploading a file does — gives Claude direct context, a shortcut to your situation — and name a few supported types: PDF, DOCX, CSV, TXT, PNG, JPEG.

sections.heading: The first reply is the start, not the answer
sections.challenge: Your first answer isn't right. Name three ways to fix it without starting over.
body: A response you don't love isn't a failure — it's a draft to push on. Three moves cover almost everything. Ask a follow-up: "expand on the second point," "make it more concise." Give feedback: "tone's too formal, make it conversational." Or redirect — steer it back, or start fresh if the chat has wandered too far to save.

There's also the pencil icon on any message you sent. Click it to edit your original prompt and resubmit, rather than piling a correction on top. That refines the request at the source instead of making Claude reconcile two competing instructions. The real value of Claude shows up over a back-and-forth, not in a single perfect prompt.
sections.plain: Editing your prompt with the pencil is fixing the recipe; sending another message is seasoning the dish after it's plated.
sections.lockIt: Refine a response three ways — a follow-up question, direct feedback, and the pencil-edit on your own message — and say when to start a fresh chat instead (when the conversation has derailed).

sections.heading: Every bad output has a named fix
sections.challenge: The output came back generic and bland. What's the first thing you change?
body: When the result disappoints, the cause is usually one of five, and each has a move. Too generic means you didn't give enough context — add your role, audience, and constraints. Wrong length means Claude is guessing — state it: "two paragraphs," "under 100 words." Wrong format means it understood the what but not the how — show, don't tell: give a format example. Tone off — describe the tone plainly or paste a sample.

The one to take seriously is confident-but-wrong. Claude can produce a clean, plausible answer that's flat false, most often on specific or niche facts. It won't sound unsure. So for anything high-stakes, verify it independently — ask for sources, ask for a confidence level, turn on web search. The fluent phrasing is not evidence the fact is right.
sections.plain: "Too generic" is a context problem, not a Claude problem — and a confident wrong answer reads exactly like a confident right one, which is the trap.
sections.lockIt: Given "the output is too generic," name the first fix — add context: audience, role, constraints — and state the rule for confident-but-wrong: verify high-stakes facts independently, because fluent doesn't mean correct.

sections.heading: AI Fluency is four competencies, not button-clicking
sections.challenge: Name the four competencies of AI Fluency.
body: AI Fluency is the ability to work well with AI as a matter of judgment, not knowing which buttons to press. It breaks into four D's. Delegation: deciding what you keep versus what you hand off. Description: communicating the task clearly — this is exactly where Stage/Task/Rules lives. Discernment: judging the output critically, where troubleshooting and evals belong. Diligence: using AI responsibly, staying transparent and accountable for the result.

The framework comes from Profs. Rick Dakan at Ringling and Joseph Feller at University College Cork, and Anthropic's free AI Fluency course goes deeper. The reason to memorize the four is that they map cleanly onto everything else in this module — most of what you're learning is one of these four wearing a different hat.
sections.plain: Delegation decides the job, Description hands it over, Discernment checks the work, Diligence owns the outcome — the same four steps you'd use handing work to a new hire.
sections.lockIt: Name all four 4D competencies — Delegation, Description, Discernment, Diligence — and place Stage/Task/Rules under Description.

sections.heading: Test it on work you've already done
sections.challenge: How would you decide whether to trust Claude on a task you do every week?
body: An eval is a plain way to test how well Claude does the specific tasks that matter to you — no infrastructure, no code. Four steps. Gather 5 to 10 real examples of a task you do often. Write prompts that should reproduce them, with your natural context. Compare the output against what you know is right — did it catch the key info, nail the tone, miss anything. Then refine: tighten the prompt, add an example of what good looks like, or flag where a human has to review.

This lives under Discernment, and it's how you turn a vague "Claude seems good at this" into earned confidence on a recurring task. You're not testing Claude in the abstract. You're testing it on your emails, your reports, your analyses — the things you'll actually delegate.
sections.plain: It's a road test on a route you already know by heart — only then can you tell whether the directions it gives are any good.
sections.lockIt: Walk the four-step eval — gather 5-10 known examples, write test prompts, compare outputs against your known-good results, refine — and say which 4D competency it sits under (Discernment).

sections.heading: Chat, Cowork, Code — one app, three gears
sections.challenge: Chat, Cowork, Code — say what each one is for.
body: The desktop app has three modes. Chat is the same Claude as the web, plus native extras — quick entry, screenshot and window sharing, dictation. Best for questions, brainstorming, drafting, back-and-forth. Cowork is agentic: you give it a goal, connect some tools, and let it run — research, cross-source analysis, finished deliverables, with a reviewable plan and visible progress. Code is Claude Code inside the app: a full dev environment that reads, writes, and runs commands in your codebase, with diffs, a terminal, and git.

Here's the part that surprises people. Cowork and Code run on the same engine — both are Claude Code underneath. Same machinery for working independently and sustaining long tasks; Cowork just points it at general deliverables instead of a codebase.
sections.plain: Chat is a conversation, Cowork is a contractor you hand a project, Code is a contractor working inside your actual code — and the last two share one engine.
sections.lockIt: Name the three modes and what each is for — Chat for quick exchanges, Cowork for sustained agentic deliverables, Code for building software — and state that Cowork and Code both run on Claude Code underneath.

sections.heading: What only each mode can do
sections.challenge: Which mode runs scheduled tasks? Which one has Ask / Code / Plan?
body: Each mode owns a few features. Chat's are about speed and your computer: quick entry (double-tap Option on Mac for an overlay), dictation, screenshots. Cowork's are about working unattended — scheduled tasks that run on a recurring clock (a daily briefing, weekly roundup, inbox triage, catching up if the app was closed), subagents that work in parallel and get merged into one deliverable, folder access, and Dispatch to continue a task from your phone.

Code's distinguishing features are about how much it does on its own and where it runs. The Ask / Code / Plan interaction modes set the leash: Ask proposes every change and waits, Code applies file edits automatically but checks before terminal commands, Plan outlines the whole approach before touching anything. And it runs local — a folder on your machine — or remote — a GitHub repo in the cloud, where a session keeps going after you close the app.
sections.plain: Scheduled tasks are Cowork's alone, Ask/Code/Plan is Code's alone — the unique features tell you what each mode was actually built to do.
sections.lockIt: Match a signature feature to its mode — quick entry to Chat, scheduled tasks to Cowork, Ask/Code/Plan and local-vs-remote dev to Code.

sections.heading: Pick by the size of the job, not the topic
sections.challenge: 'Read 50 contracts, write a memo' — which mode, and why?
body: The mode you want is set by how big the job is, not what it's about. A quick back-and-forth — a question, a draft, a brainstorm — that's Chat. A large autonomous deliverable you want Claude to go off and produce — research compiled, sources analyzed, a finished memo — that's Cowork. Building or changing software — write it, run it, commit it — that's Code.

The trap is reading the topic and stopping there. "50 contracts, summarize them into a memo" sounds like a Chat job because it's just reading. It isn't. It's a big, multi-step deliverable that runs while you do something else — that's Cowork. Ask the size question first: quick exchange, autonomous deliverable, or code.
sections.plain: The signal is the size of the job, not the subject — fifty contracts into a memo is a Cowork job even though no code is anywhere near it.
sections.lockIt: For "summarize 50 contracts into a memo," pick Cowork and justify it by job size — a large autonomous deliverable, not a quick exchange (Chat) or software (Code).

nugget.title: The delegation–diligence loop: trust is something you test for
nugget.challenge: Why would you test Claude on a task you already know the answer to?
body: Here's the move that separates people who actually rely on AI from people who half-trust it and re-do everything anyway. Before you delegate a task, test Claude on a version where you already know the right answer. Pick a real task you do often. Find past data where you've already done the analysis — you need the known-correct result to grade against. Have Claude reproduce it. Check what it produced, how it got there, what it missed. Refine your prompt, test again. If it matches your known result, you can trust it for similar work going forward. If you can't get there after several honest refinements, you've learned this is a task you shouldn't hand off.

Watch Rio, a program director, run it. He analyzes attendance against employment outcomes every quarter — hours of cleaning messy data and wrangling formulas. He tests Claude on last quarter's data, which he's already solved. Claude nails the attendance-to-placement correlation but misses a combined housing-assistance and job-placement insight. He refines — "pay special attention to the program type" — and Claude catches it. Now he has a validated, repeatable approach and a note: future quarters must specify program type and include enrollment dates. He's working from validated confidence, not guesswork.
nugget.whyItMatters: This is how you decide what to delegate without gambling on real stakes. You buy confidence with a task you can grade, then spend it on the tasks you can't. And it draws the honest line: validation builds confidence but never removes responsibility — you still own checking the numbers make sense and being transparent about Claude's role. The lock that makes it master-level: trust isn't a feeling, it's a defined, scoped test against a concrete objective target — the same way a fix gets validated before it ships.
nugget.lockIt: Explain why you'd test Claude on data you already know the answer to — to grade its output against a known-correct result before trusting it on live work — and mark this true or false: "validation removes your responsibility for the result." (False — it builds confidence but you stay accountable.)

outro: Step back and the whole module is one idea wearing four hats. Claude generates rather than retrieves, so the context you feed it is the whole game — Stage/Task/Rules, uploaded files, follow-ups, the right mode for the size of the job. Every objective here is really about getting the right material in front of it and then judging what comes back.

The delegation–diligence loop is where that judgment gets teeth: test on what you know, trust what passes, own the result either way. Carry that into Module 2 on Projects, and you'll be organizing context on purpose instead of re-explaining yourself every chat. The tool was never the hard part. Steering it is the skill, and you now have the bar to clear.
